title: Top Five Threequels and ‘The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It’
format: Cannes returns from its pandemic year off, Chris Ryan joins to help Amanda through 'The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It,' and all three draft their favorite third movies in a series.

films.title: Annette
films.note: Leos Carax's musical, one of the titles all three hosts are most looking forward to out of the competition lineup.

films.title: The French Dispatch of the Liberty, Kansas Evening Sun
films.note: Wes Anderson's anthology, also high on the anticipation list; Chris wonders if Cannes just bills it as 'Dispatch.'

films.title: Titane
films.note: Julia Ducournau's follow-up to her 2016 film 'Raw'; Sean calls it the movie he's most looking forward to at the festival.

films.title: Hold Me Tight
films.note: Mathieu Amalric's film, starring Vicky Krieps of 'Phantom Thread.'

films.title: Old
films.note: M. Night Shyamalan's beach-set thriller, also starring Krieps; not a Cannes title, but Sean brings it up for the connection and says he's 'really psyched' for it.

films.title: JFK Revisited: Through the Looking Glass
films.note: Oliver Stone's documentary revisiting his JFK conspiracy theories. Sean had seen the first two hours and wasn't ready to comment; runtime unconfirmed at the time of taping.

films.title: Flag Day
films.note: Sean Penn directs and stars opposite Josh Brolin and Miles Teller, playing a con-man father to a daughter played by his real-life daughter, Dylan Penn. Chris was tipped off to it months earlier and calls it tailor-made for him.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: Jane Campion's film, one of several starry titles skipping Cannes amid the festival's ongoing standoff with Netflix.

films.title: Decision to Leave
films.note: Park Chan-wook's next film, expected at Venice instead of Cannes.

films.title: The Card Counter
films.note: Paul Schrader's gambling drama, also bound for Venice; Sean calls it 'the only film that matters in 2021.'

films.title: Spencer
films.note: Pablo Larraín's Princess Diana film starring Kristen Stewart, also skipping Cannes for Venice. Chris isn't sure it's the role he'd have picked for her.

films.title: Red Rocket
films.note: Sean Baker's first film since 'The Florida Project,' playing in competition.

segments.heading: ‘The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It’
segments.note: the third Conjuring film, eighth in the franchise, new on HBO Max and in theaters

films.title: The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It
films.note: Vera Farmiga and Patrick Wilson return as Ed and Lorraine Warren for a true-crime-style case structure Chris likens to 'Mindhunter.' James Wan didn't direct this one, and both hosts feel the absence of his more patient camera. Amanda, not a horror fan, liked the connective scenes more than the scares, and singles out a possessed child punching Patrick Wilson in the film's opening minutes.

films.title: The Conjuring
films.note: James Wan's original, praised by Sean for withholding scares and centering Lily Taylor's performance as a mother slowly possessed.

films.title: The Conjuring 2
films.note: The other Wan-directed entry, which Chris also rates highly.

films.title: Malignant
films.note: James Wan's next horror movie, unseen by either host but reportedly 'very upsetting.'

films.title: The Devil and Father Amorth
films.note: William Friedkin's documentary featuring a real-life exorcism; Chris assigns it to Amanda as homework.

films.title: The Dark Knight Rises
films.note: Sean's reference point for the self-conscious 'everything ends' marketing that trilogy-closers lean on, even when a franchise obviously isn't done.

films.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
films.note: Cited as a cautionary tale about not planning a trilogy ahead of time; Sean calls it 'quite a bad film.'

segments.heading: Amanda's Top Five Threequels
segments.note: ranked worst to best on air, best-first here
segments.subject: Threequels

films.title: Before Midnight
films.note: No. 1. Richard Linklater's third 'Before' film, with Ethan Hawke and Julie Delpy. Amanda calls sticking the landing on a marriage-in-crisis story, after two much lighter films, 'a stunning accomplishment.'

films.title: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Chris and Sean. Sean Connery's performance as Indy's father is the reason it edges out 'Raiders' for her.

films.title: Toy Story 3
films.note: No. 3. Picked partly to argue Pixar's third installment doubles as an effective horror movie, incinerator sequence included.

films.title: The Last Days of Disco
films.note: No. 4. Whit Stillman's third film, loosely grouped with 'Metropolitan' and 'Barcelona' as a trilogy connected by theme and actor Chris Eigeman rather than continuity. Stars Kate Beckinsale and Chloë Sevigny as disaffected New York yuppies.

films.title: National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation
films.note: No. 5. The third film in the Vacation series; Amanda saw it before the first two as a kid and calls it a childhood touchstone, citing the attic-ladder scene and Chevy Chase's home-repair disasters.

segments.heading: Chris's Top Five Threequels
segments.note: ranked worst to best on air, best-first here
segments.subject: Threequels

films.title: Die Hard with a Vengeance
films.note: No. 1. John McClane and Zeus Carver race around New York solving a terrorist's Simon Says puzzles. Originally an unrelated script called 'Simon Says' retrofitted into the franchise.

films.title: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Amanda and Sean, all three agree it's the rare threequel that improves on trying to just repeat the formula.

films.title: Scream 3
films.note: No. 3. A meta commentary on making the final film of a horror trilogy; Chris highlights Parker Posey's performance as Gale Weathers playing Gale Weathers within the film's movie-within-a-movie.

films.title: Mad Max Beyond Thunderdome
films.note: No. 4. George Miller's expansion of the post-apocalyptic world, with Tina Turner as Aunty Entity, that predates 'Fury Road' by decades and inspired the 'California Love' music video.

films.title: The Exorcist III
films.note: No. 5. Directed by William Peter Blatty, who wrote the source novels, with George C. Scott investigating a serial killer 20 years after the original. Chris calls its jump scare one of the best in movie history.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Threequels
segments.note: ranked worst to best on air, best-first here
segments.subject: Threequels

films.title: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
films.note: No. 1. Sergio Leone's conclusion to the Man With No Name trilogy, with Clint Eastwood, Lee Van Cleef and Eli Wallach. Sean cites Ennio Morricone's score as one of the greatest in film history.

films.title: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Amanda and Chris.

films.title: Mission: Impossible III
films.note: No. 3. J.J. Abrams' divisive entry, with Philip Seymour Hoffman as the villain and a Vatican break-in as its centerpiece set piece.

films.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
films.note: No. 4. The conclusion of a trilogy shot together as one planned production, which Sean says makes its many endings forgivable.

films.title: Die Hard with a Vengeance
films.note: No. 5. Ranked lower than Chris has it, but Sean still calls Jeremy Irons 'fucking awesome' as the villain.

segments.heading: Honorable Mentions
segments.note: the ones that didn't make the cut

films.title: 16 Blocks
films.note: Richard Donner's low-key near-Die Hard, with Bruce Willis as a burnt-out cop walking a witness played by Mos Def sixteen blocks to a courthouse. Chris calls it 'basically Die Hard 4.'

films.title: Clear and Present Danger
films.note: The third Jack Ryan film, and the second to recast the role, from Alec Baldwin to Harrison Ford. Sean calls it a 'significant jump up' from 'Patriot Games.'

referenced.group: Cannes: a run through past Palme d'Or winners
